</imports>

<findings>

[observation] [warning] 10 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
[observation] [warning] High coupling (0.54) — 43 imports target "analyze-engine"
[observation] [warning] 43 imports from unit-analyze and 26 imports back (bidirectional) indicate the CLI layer and analysis engine are mutually entangled. Introducing a clear command→domain dependency direction (CLI calls domain, never the reverse) would improve layering.
[observation] [info] The presence of a dedicated output.ts and errors.ts at src/cli/ is a positive pattern — centralizing output formatting and error presentation keeps individual command files focused on orchestration logic.
[observation] [info] stats.ts, timestamps.ts, and verify.ts being co-located with CLI commands suggests some domain utilities have drifted into the CLI zone — these may belong in the analyze engine or graph utilities zone.
[suggestion] [info] Zone "cli-command-layer" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
[suggestion] [info] The -layer suffix on cli-command-layer is inconsistent with all other zone names. Adopt a uniform naming convention (either all zones use tier suffixes or none do) and document it in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md.
[suggestion] [warning] Zone ID unit-cli does not match display name cli-command-layer. Align IDs with display names across all three triadic zones simultaneously to avoid partial fixes that leave the translation problem intact.
[suggestion] [info] stats.ts and timestamps.ts have zone-neutral names that provide no navigational signal for developers. When relocating them (to analyze-engine or a shared utilities module), rename to prd-stats.ts and prd-timestamps.ts to make domain affiliation explicit in the filename.
[relationship] [warning] cli-command-layer depends on the low-cohesion cli zone (0.22) for foundation utilities — stabilizing cli zone internals would reduce downstream risk for cli-command-layer without requiring changes to cli-command-layer itself.
[anti-pattern] [warning] stats.ts and timestamps.ts are pure-computation utilities co-located with CLI command files. Their presence in the CLI zone inflates coupling and suppresses cohesion — they should be relocated to analyze-engine or a shared utility module.
[anti-pattern] [warning] token-format.ts and usage.ts implement token-count presentation with no shared contract to the hench package's actual token-counting logic. If the two implementations diverge, the usage dashboard and actual agent consumption will report different numbers with no type system or lint enforcement to catch the drift.

</findings>

<insights>

- High coupling (0.54) — 43 imports target "analyze-engine"
- 10 entry points — wide API surface, consider consolidating exports
- Moderate cohesion (0.46) and coupling (0.54) suggest the CLI layer is cohesive enough but draws broadly from the analyze engine and store — expected for a presentation layer
- status-sections.ts and status-shared.ts indicate the status command has been decomposed into composable display units, which is a good maintainability pattern
- token-format.ts and usage.ts together handle the usage/cost reporting surface; confirm these are wired to the same token-counting logic used by hench to avoid reporting drift
- 43 imports from unit-analyze and 26 imports back (bidirectional) indicate the CLI layer and analysis engine are mutually entangled. Introducing a clear command→domain dependency direction (CLI calls domain, never the reverse) would improve layering.
- stats.ts, timestamps.ts, and verify.ts being co-located with CLI commands suggests some domain utilities have drifted into the CLI zone — these may belong in the analyze engine or graph utilities zone.
- The presence of a dedicated output.ts and errors.ts at src/cli/ is a positive pattern — centralizing output formatting and error presentation keeps individual command files focused on orchestration logic.
- Zone "cli-command-layer" has files across 6 directories — consider consolidating under a dedicated directory
- cli-command-layer imports 12 times from the cli zone (cohesion 0.22) and also imports from analyze-engine, prd-graph-utilities, and store simultaneously — it has the widest cross-zone consumer footprint of any zone, touching 4 distinct upstream zones.
- cli-command-layer depends on the low-cohesion cli zone (0.22) for foundation utilities — stabilizing cli zone internals would reduce downstream risk for cli-command-layer without requiring changes to cli-command-layer itself.
- token-format.ts and usage.ts in the CLI layer handle token-count presentation independently of the hench package's token-counting logic. There is no shared type contract or shared counting function between the two — any divergence in how tokens are counted or displayed between the CLI usage command and hench's actual consumption tracking will silently produce inconsistent numbers in dashboards.
- stats.ts and timestamps.ts being co-located with CLI commands is a concrete misplaced-file instance: these are pure-computation utilities with no output formatting concerns, and their presence in the CLI zone suppresses the zone's cohesion score while adding unnecessary coupling to the CLI presentation layer.
- token-format.ts and usage.ts implement token-count presentation with no shared contract to the hench package's actual token-counting logic. If the two implementations diverge, the usage dashboard and actual agent consumption will report different numbers with no type system or lint enforcement to catch the drift.
- stats.ts and timestamps.ts are pure-computation utilities co-located with CLI command files. Their presence in the CLI zone inflates coupling and suppresses cohesion — they should be relocated to analyze-engine or a shared utility module.
- Zone ID unit-cli does not match display name cli-command-layer — same dual naming inconsistency as the other triadic zones. All three triadic participants (analyze-engine, cli-command-layer, prd-graph-utilities) have mismatched IDs and display names, suggesting the renaming was applied at the display layer only and the underlying zone topology data was not updated.
- The -layer suffix in cli-command-layer is unique among all zone names. No other zone appends an architectural tier label as a suffix (analyze-engine does not say analyze-engine-domain, prd-graph-utilities does not say prd-graph-utilities-utility). The asymmetric naming implies CLI commands occupy a special architectural category while other zones do not — a convention that is not documented and creates ambiguity about whether future zones should also use tier suffixes.
- stats.ts and timestamps.ts are pure-computation utilities in the CLI zone but neither filename signals CLI affiliation — they would be equally at home in analyze-engine or a shared utilities module. Their current names create no navigational friction when they drift into the wrong zone, because the names carry no zone-affiliation signal.
- Zone ID unit-cli does not match display name cli-command-layer. Align IDs with display names across all three triadic zones simultaneously to avoid partial fixes that leave the translation problem intact.
- The -layer suffix on cli-command-layer is inconsistent with all other zone names. Adopt a uniform naming convention (either all zones use tier suffixes or none do) and document it in PACKAGE_GUIDELINES.md.
- stats.ts and timestamps.ts have zone-neutral names that provide no navigational signal for developers. When relocating them (to analyze-engine or a shared utilities module), rename to prd-stats.ts and prd-timestamps.ts to make domain affiliation explicit in the filename.
- [call graph] 1091 internal calls, 98 outgoing, 413 incoming (cohesion: 0.92, coupling: 0.08)

</insights>
